Idiom (Ndyuka): Ala pii tifi a ná lafu.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed proverbial negative equative clause: universal quantifier + noun phrase + a ná + predicate noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Appearances can deceive; a smile, show of friendliness, or polite surface may hide another intention.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: No ala sama di e lafu gi yu abi bun ati.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; family advice; politics; conflict mediation; cautionary humor
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Okanisi nongo speech tradition; used in veiled criticism and kuutu-style moral instruction.
First Example Sentence: Te a man e pii tifi gi yu, no feti; ala pii tifi a ná lafu.
Second Example Sentence: Na a kuutu mi be membe den taki: ala pii tifi a ná lafu.
Third Example Sentence: Den pikin e lafu, ma a mma sabi taki ala pii tifi a ná lafu.

Idiom (Ndyuka): Wan boto ná abi tu sitiman.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed negative possessive proverb: numeral subject + noun + ná abi + numeral object compound.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A task, family, meeting, or organization needs clear leadership; divided command causes disorder.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wan sani mu abi wan sama di e tyari en.
Usage Contexts: leadership; workplace; kuutu; politics; river travel; project management
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: River-boat leadership metaphor from Eastern Maroon life along the Marowijne and Tapanahony waterways.
First Example Sentence: Na a wooko ya u mu meki wan basi; wan boto ná abi tu sitiman.
Second Example Sentence: Efi den tu gaaman wani tii a sani, da mi taki: wan boto ná abi tu sitiman.
Third Example Sentence: Na a liba a boto no o waka bun te wan boto abi tu sitiman.

Idiom (Ndyuka): A wan boto, a ná a fa a bigi.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Topic-comment proverb: copular topic phrase followed by negative evaluative clause with manner marker fa.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Value is not decided by size, rank, or appearance; usefulness and direction matter more.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: No a bigi fu a sani namo e sori en warti.
Usage Contexts: workplace; education; family comparison; leadership; humility; encouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Boat-image nongo used to redirect attention from outward size to actual performance and role.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ndyuka): [a wan bo.to a ná a fa a bi.gi]
First Example Sentence: No luku a oso bigi ete; a wan boto, a ná a fa a bigi.
Second Example Sentence: A pikin boto tja u kaba, bika a wan boto, a ná a fa a bigi.
Third Example Sentence: Na wooko, a ná titili di e tyari sani; a wan boto, a ná a fa a bigi.

Idiom (Ndyuka): Luku fa a masini e daai de.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperative idiom: verb luku + embedded manner clause with imperfective marker e.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Study how the whole situation is functioning before judging or intervening.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Luku fa a wooko e go bifo i meki piki.
Usage Contexts: technical work; conflict mediation; business; politics; elder advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Motorboat and machine imagery in riverine communities; used as a practical metaphor for systems and leadership.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ndyuka): [lu.ku fa a ma.si.ni e daːi de]
First Example Sentence: Bifo u koti a moni, luku fa a masini e daai de.
Second Example Sentence: Mi no e piki te den taki switi; mi e luku fa a masini e daai de.
Third Example Sentence: A takiman no kii kuutu; luku fa a masini e daai de.

Idiom (Ndyuka): A wan kodo man e tii en.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Focus construction: copular subject + singular focused noun phrase + imperfective verb phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: One accountable person must steer a matter, even when many people are involved.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Wan sama mu tyari a sani fu meki pasi tan bun.
Usage Contexts: team coordination; community meetings; family decisions; governance; boat travel
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Steering metaphor from canoes and river transport, extended to social authority and responsibility.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ndyuka): [a wan ko.do man e tiː eŋ]
First Example Sentence: Den man e taki tuutuu, ma a wan kodo man e tii en.
Second Example Sentence: Na a botopasi, a wan kodo man e tii en, da ala sama mu tan leisi.
Third Example Sentence: Efi u wani kaba a wooko, meki wan sama tyari ede, bika a wan kodo man e tii en.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Metaphorical copular noun-predicate clause: first-person subject + na + plant/tuber noun.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: I am adaptable and will root or continue only where conditions allow me to thrive.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mi kan tan pe doti bun gi mi, ma mi kan gwé te a no bun.
Usage Contexts: dispute resolution; migration; workplace fit; family pressure; self-positioning
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Agricultural root-crop metaphor; connected to planting, soil, mobility, and belonging in forest-river life.
First Example Sentence: No fasi mi wan pe, mi na napi, mi o beli pe doti bun.
Second Example Sentence: Mi na napi; pe den paandi mi ma doti no bun, mi e gwé.
Third Example Sentence: A wookoman taki mi na napi bika mi e feni pasi pe sani kan go.

Idiom (Ndyuka): Pe den e paandi mi, mi ná e beli de.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Locative subordinate clause followed by negative imperfective main clause; metaphor of planting and rooting.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: I do not become committed merely because others place me somewhere; belonging requires suitable conditions.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mi no e tan pe wan kondi no de fu mi.
Usage Contexts: arbitration; employment; relocation; family decisions; identity negotiation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Napi/planting-ground image used in formal speech to discuss participation without direct confrontation.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ndyuka): [pe deŋ e paːn.di mi mi ná e be.li de]
First Example Sentence: Mi lobi a kondre, ma pe den e paandi mi, mi ná e beli de.
Second Example Sentence: Te wan basi no gi gwenti, mi taki: pe den e paandi mi, mi ná e beli de.
Third Example Sentence: A uman kaba a wooko de bika pe den e paandi en, en ná e beli de.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Copular predicate-nominal identity clause with agentive compound wakaman.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: I am a mobile person or free agent; I am not permanently tied to one place, camp, or side.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mi no de wan pe nomo; mi e waka suku pe mi kan du bun.
Usage Contexts: travel; migration; work search; informal identity talk; humorous self-description
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects movement across river villages, coastal towns, French Guiana, Suriname, and diaspora spaces.
First Example Sentence: Mi a wakaman, no poti mi na wan taki no abi ede.
Second Example Sentence: Den taki mi fu tan, ma mi a wakaman te mi feni bun pisi doti.
Third Example Sentence: Na wan wakaman mi de; di pasi opo, mi e go.

Idiom (Ndyuka): A án mu kon fu Anansi anga Sensen.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative modal-result clause with complementizer fu and paired folktale proper names.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A serious plan should not become trickery, confusion, or divided intention disguised as cooperation.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: U no mu meki koni anga winsi meki a pikin sani broko.
Usage Contexts: folktale allusion; politics; community leadership; moral warning; sarcasm
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Alludes to Anansi/Sensen trickster narrative traditions used in Maroon formal speech and moral reasoning.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Ndyuka): [a án mu koŋ fu a.nan.si aŋ.ga sen.sen]
First Example Sentence: U taki u o du leti, so a án mu kon fu Anansi anga Sensen.
Second Example Sentence: A kuutu na fu bun fasi; a án mu kon fu Anansi anga Sensen.
Third Example Sentence: Te wan man e kisi ede anga koni, mi e taagi en taki a án mu kon fu Anansi anga Sensen.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Habitual/general present clause with imperfective marker e and purpose/manner complement fu leti.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: We act properly, honestly, and in the right way; the work must be done with moral correctness.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: U mu du a sani anga leti ati.
Usage Contexts: family instruction; school; workplace ethics; kuutu; public responsibility
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kuutu moral formula emphasizing right conduct, accountability, and truthful communal action.
First Example Sentence: Te u piki a lanti, u e du fu leti.
Second Example Sentence: No anga libi-libi taki; u e du fu leti na ini kuutu.
Third Example Sentence: A pikin taki: mi o gi a moni baka, bika u e du fu leti.
